devotional.bigIdea: God can send fire from heaven and also bring bread to a prophet who has collapsed in the wilderness. His power and His gentleness always come together.
questions: Elijah soaked the altar before asking for fire, making the miracle obviously God's work. Why do you think he did that?
After the greatest moment of his life, Elijah wanted to give up. Have you ever felt a crash after a big effort?
God fed Elijah before He spoke to him. What does that say about how God treats people who are exhausted and broken?

stories.title: The Lion's Den
stories.hook: They passed a law specifically designed to make Daniel's faith illegal. He prayed anyway.
story: Jerusalem had fallen. The Babylonians had destroyed the Temple and marched the people of Israel into exile. Among the first group taken was a young man named Daniel, chosen for his intelligence to be trained in the language and literature of Babylon and serve in the royal court.

Daniel served faithfully through the entire Babylonian empire and into the next one, the Persian empire under Darius the Mede. He was by this point an old man, and Darius was planning to give him authority over the entire kingdom. This made the other administrators furious. They tried to find grounds for charges against Daniel in his conduct of government — and could find nothing. He was trustworthy, neither corrupt nor negligent.

They went to Darius and flattered him into issuing a decree: anyone who prays to any god or human being except Darius during the next thirty days will be thrown into the lion's den. Darius signed it into law.

Daniel heard about the decree. He went home to his upstairs room where his windows opened toward Jerusalem, and he did exactly what he had always done: three times a day, he knelt and prayed and gave thanks to God. In front of the open window. Where anyone could see. He did not hide. He did not modify. He prayed.

The administrators found him praying and ran to Darius. The king was distressed and worked all day trying to find a way to rescue Daniel. He could not. At sunset, Daniel was thrown into the lion's den, and a stone was rolled over the entrance. Darius said to him: May your God, whom you serve continually, rescue you.

The king went back to his palace. He could not eat. He could not sleep. At the first light of dawn, he ran to the den and called out: Daniel, servant of the living God, has your God been able to rescue you from the lions? And from inside came an old man's voice: My God sent His angel and shut the mouths of the lions. They have not hurt me, because I was found innocent in His sight.

Daniel was lifted out of the den unharmed. The men who had accused him were thrown in instead. And Darius wrote to every nation in his kingdom: I issue a decree that in every part of my kingdom people must fear and reverence the God of Daniel. For He is the living God and He endures forever. He rescues and He saves.
Daniel did not pray because it was safe. He prayed because it was true. Real faith does not change its behavior based on whether anyone is watching — or what they will do if they see.

devotional.bigIdea: Daniel did not pray because it was safe. He prayed because it was true. Real faith does not change its behavior based on who is watching.
questions: Daniel prayed in front of the open window even knowing people were watching for exactly that. What would you have done?
Darius spent the whole night unable to sleep, hoping Daniel's God would save him. What does that tell you about what Daniel's life had communicated about who God was?
Where in your life do you feel pressure to hide your faith or change your behavior to fit in?

stories.title: For Such a Time as This
stories.hook: A Jewish girl became queen of Persia and had to decide whether to risk her life to save her people.
story: The Jewish people were scattered across the Persian empire, and a young Jewish woman named Esther had been raised by her older cousin Mordecai after the death of her parents. When King Ahasuerus sought a new queen, Esther was chosen above all others. She became queen of the most powerful empire in the world.

Among the king's officials was a man named Haman, elevated to the highest position. Everyone bowed before him — everyone except Mordecai. Mordecai refused. Haman burned with hatred, and when he found out Mordecai was Jewish, he decided that destroying one man was not enough. He would destroy all the Jews in the entire empire.

Haman persuaded the king, and dispatches went to every province: in eleven months, all Jews — young and old, women and children — were to be killed in a single day. Mordecai tore his clothes and mourned. Word reached Esther. She sent a servant to find out what was wrong. Mordecai sent back a message asking her to go to the king and plead for her people.

Esther sent back what was almost a refusal: anyone who approaches the king in the inner court without being summoned is put to death — unless the king holds out the golden scepter. I have not been summoned in thirty days.

Mordecai's reply has echoed across three thousand years: Do not think that because you are in the king's house you alone of all the Jews will escape. If you remain silent at this time, relief and deliverance for the Jews will arise from another place, but you and your father's family will perish. And who knows whether you have not come to the kingdom for such a time as this?

Esther asked all the Jews in Susa to fast for three days on her behalf. Then she said something that takes your breath away: I will go to the king, even though it is against the law. And if I perish, I perish. On the third day, she went to the inner court. The king saw her. He held out the golden scepter.

Through a brilliantly patient strategy, Esther revealed Haman's plot to the king at exactly the right moment. Haman was hanged on the very gallows he had built for Mordecai. The Jews were given the right to defend themselves and were victorious.

The book of Esther is the only book in the Bible that never mentions God by name. But His fingerprints are on every page. Providence — God arranging circumstances through ordinary human choices and courage and timing — is woven through every scene. A girl became queen. A man refused to bow. A woman said if I perish, I perish. And a people was saved.

devotional.bigIdea: God places people in exactly the right position at exactly the right time. For such a time as this is not just Esther's story — it might be yours too.
questions: Mordecai told Esther she might have come to her position for such a time as this. Do you think God puts people in places on purpose? Where might He have put you on purpose?
Esther said if I perish, I perish — she had already counted the cost. What does it look like to make a decision that is right even if it costs you?
The book of Esther never mentions God by name. Does that make His presence in the story more or less powerful to you?

stories.title: Impossible News
stories.hook: A teenage girl in a forgotten village got a visit from an angel. What she said next would change everything.
story: Nazareth was not on anyone's list of important places. It was a small hillside village in Galilee — the northern region of Israel that the more sophisticated Judeans in Jerusalem looked down on — with perhaps four or five hundred residents, no notable history, no strategic position on any trade route, no connection to any prophecy that religious scholars were actively watching. Can anything good come out of Nazareth? someone would ask later in the story, and it would be meant as a serious question.

Mary lived there. She was young — in first-century Jewish culture, girls were typically betrothed in their early to mid-teens — engaged to a carpenter named Joseph from the house and lineage of David. She was, by every visible measure, an ordinary young woman in an ordinary village preparing for an ordinary life. And then the angel Gabriel appeared to her.

Gabriel is one of only two angels named in the canonical Scriptures. He appears at pivotal moments in the biblical narrative: he explained Daniel's visions, he announced the birth of John the Baptist to Zechariah in the Temple. Now he came to a teenage girl in Galilee, and his greeting alone was enough to unsettle her: Greetings, you who are highly favored! The Lord is with you. The text says Mary was greatly troubled at his words and wondered what kind of greeting this might be. Not the sight of an angel — the greeting. She was thinking carefully about what those words meant.

Gabriel continued: Do not be afraid, Mary; you have found favor with God. You will conceive and give birth to a son, and you are to call him Jesus. He will be great and will be called the Son of the Most High. The Lord God will give him the throne of his father David, and he will reign over Jacob's descendants forever; his kingdom will never end.

Mary's response was not doubt — it was a practical question about biology: How will this be, since I am a virgin? Gabriel explained that the Holy Spirit would come upon her and the power of the Most High would overshadow her. Therefore the holy one to be born would be called the Son of God. He added: Even Elizabeth your relative has conceived a son in her old age. And then the sentence that has steadied every believer who has ever faced an impossible situation: For no word from God will ever fail.

Mary's answer is one of the most remarkable acts of faith in all of Scripture. She did not ask for more signs. She did not negotiate. She said: I am the Lord's servant. May your word to me be fulfilled. She consented to something that she knew would be misunderstood by everyone around her. An unmarried pregnant woman in first-century Jewish society faced severe social consequences. She said yes anyway. The hinge of all human history turned on one teenage girl's act of trust.

Joseph, when he found out, planned to divorce her quietly to avoid public humiliation. He was a righteous man who did not want to expose her to public disgrace. But an angel appeared to him in a dream and confirmed everything: the child conceived in Mary was from the Holy Spirit. He was to name the child Jesus, which means the Lord saves, because he would save his people from their sins. Joseph woke up and did what the angel commanded. His quiet, immediate obedience is one of the often-overlooked acts of faith in the Christmas story.

The birth itself happened not in Nazareth but in Bethlehem — the city of David, ninety miles to the south — because Caesar Augustus had decreed a census of the entire Roman world and everyone had to register in their ancestral hometown. God arranged the logistics of the most powerful empire on earth to fulfill a prophecy Micah had written seven hundred years earlier: But you, Bethlehem Ephrathah, though you are small among the clans of Judah, out of you will come for me one who will be ruler over Israel, whose origins are from of old, from ancient times.

They arrived in Bethlehem with the birth imminent and found no room in the guest quarters, already filled with other travelers. The family took shelter in the place where the animals were kept — most likely a cave stable or a lower room of a house — and there, the Word became flesh. The eternal Son of God, through whom all things were made, entered human history as a helpless newborn who needed to be fed and held and kept warm.

He was wrapped in cloth strips and placed in a manger — a feeding trough for animals. The contrast is deliberate and staggering: the One who called the universe into existence with a word now lay in a stone box used to hold grain for livestock. This is the Incarnation — not a lesser version of God slipping on a human costume, but the fullness of God taking on real human flesh, with all its fragility and dependency, because there was no other way to do what needed to be done.

That same night, in fields outside Bethlehem, shepherds were keeping watch over their flocks. Shepherds were considered among the lowest social classes in first-century Jewish society. God chose them for the first announcement. An angel appeared, the glory of the Lord blazed around them, and they were terrified. Do not be afraid. I bring you good news that will cause great joy for all the people. Today in the town of David a Savior has been born to you; he is the Messiah, the Lord. Then the whole sky filled with the heavenly host, praising God: Glory to God in the highest heaven, and on earth peace to those on whom his favor rests.

The shepherds ran to Bethlehem and found everything exactly as the angel had described. They told Mary and Joseph what they had been told. Then they went back to their fields, glorifying and praising God. Mary treasured all these things and pondered them in her heart. The girl from Nazareth who had said yes to the impossible was holding the fulfillment of four thousand years of promises, wrapped in cloth, lying in a manger. God had come.

devotional.bigIdea: Mary said yes to something she could not fully understand, knowing it would cost her. God chose the forgotten village, the ordinary girl, the animal stable, and the shepherds — because His glory does not need the world's platforms.
questions: Mary said I am the Lord's servant before she knew how any of it would work out. What does that level of trust actually look like in a real person's life?
God arranged a Roman census to fulfill a seven-hundred-year-old prophecy. What does that tell you about how far ahead God is working in the details of history?
Why do you think God announced the birth first to shepherds — the people with the lowest social standing — rather than to priests or kings?

stories.title: Left Behind in Jerusalem
stories.hook: Jesus was twelve years old when His parents lost Him for three terrifying days — and found Him exactly where He said He would be.
story: Every year, Jewish families who could make the journey traveled to Jerusalem for Passover. The Feast of Passover was the most important of the three pilgrimage festivals commanded in the Law — a week-long celebration remembering the night God delivered Israel from Egypt, when the blood of the lamb on the doorframes protected every household marked with it. The journey from Nazareth to Jerusalem was approximately ninety miles, a walk of three to four days. Joseph and Mary made this journey every year, faithful to the Law, faithful to the tradition.

When Jesus was twelve years old, they made the journey as usual with the group of relatives and neighbors forming the Galilean pilgrimage caravan. Large groups traveled together for safety and community — children moving freely between family clusters, adults catching up with people they might not see any other time of year. The week of Passover in Jerusalem would have been extraordinary — the city's population swelling from perhaps eighty thousand to several hundred thousand pilgrims, the Temple courts packed, the sound of prayer and sacrifice filling the air from before dawn.

The festival ended. The caravan turned north for home. Joseph and Mary traveled a full day's journey before they looked for Jesus among their relatives and friends — and could not find him. The day had been long, the group was large, the assumption natural. A twelve-year-old boy in a caravan of trusted community members was not a cause for alarm, until suddenly he was not where anyone expected him to be.

They turned around. A full day's journey back to Jerusalem. They arrived and spent three days searching — three days of mounting terror in a city of hundreds of thousands of people, asking everyone they could find, retracing every step of the festival week. The text does not dramatize their grief, but it does not need to. Three days. Missing child. City of strangers. Those three words are enough.

On the third day, they found him in the Temple courts. He was sitting among the teachers — the religious scholars and rabbis who used the Temple porticoes as a teaching space, engaging in the rabbinic tradition of question and answer. Jesus was not sitting in the back row, quietly listening. He was in the conversation. The text says everyone who heard him was amazed at his understanding and his answers. These were serious students of the Torah who recognized they were encountering something extraordinary in this twelve-year-old from Galilee.

Mary's response was immediate and completely human. Son, why have you treated us like this? Your father and I have been anxiously searching for you. The Greek word translated anxiously — odynao — is used elsewhere in Luke to describe the agony of the rich man in Hades and the distress of a man watching his son die. This was not mild frustration. This was the searing anxiety of a parent who has lost a child.

Jesus's answer was gentle, wondering, almost surprised. Why were you searching for me? Did you not know I had to be in my Father's house? He was not being dismissive of their grief. He was revealing something about His identity — something He apparently assumed they already understood. The Temple was not just a religious building. It was His Father's house. And where else would a son be but in his father's house?

Luke adds two important notes. First: they did not understand what he was saying to them. Even Mary, who had been told by Gabriel that this child was the Son of God, who had pondered these things in her heart for twelve years, did not fully grasp what He meant. The full understanding of who Jesus was would unfold slowly, through death and resurrection, before it became clear. Second: Jesus went back to Nazareth with them and was obedient to them.

That second note deserves careful attention. Jesus — who at twelve years old had just astonished the most educated religious minds in Israel — went home to Nazareth and was obedient to his parents. He returned to the carpenter's workshop. To the rhythms of a small village. To carrying water and working wood and eating ordinary meals at an ordinary table. For eighteen more years, nothing happened that looked like the ministry He had been born for. He grew in wisdom and stature, and in favor with God and man. He grew — meaning He developed, learned, matured, experienced the full arc of human growing up in a specific place and time and family.

This is the only story the Bible preserves from the thirty years between His birth and His baptism. Luke, the careful historian who interviewed eyewitnesses, chose this one. Because it teaches something essential: Jesus was fully God and fully human. He knew who His Father was at twelve years old. He also grew up. He also submitted. He also learned obedience — not because He needed correction, but because the Incarnation was real. He did not fast-forward through childhood to get to the important parts. He lived every year of it.

The ordinary years were not wasted years. They were preparation years. The boy who went home and was obedient would grow into the man who would say: I have come not to do my own will but the will of the Father who sent me. The pattern of willing, faithful submission was not incidental to who Jesus was. It was central to it.

devotional.bigIdea: Jesus was twelve, fully aware of who He was — and He went home and was obedient. Your ordinary, faithful, everyday life is not wasted time. It is where character is built.
questions: Jesus was twelve years old when this happened — around your age. What do you think His daily life in Nazareth was like in the years before His ministry began?

He could have stayed in Jerusalem — the scholars wanted to hear more. But He went home and was obedient. What does that tell you about the relationship between great calling and ordinary faithfulness?
Mary and Joseph did not fully understand what Jesus said. Have you ever had an experience with God that you did not fully understand at the time but understood later?

stories.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
stories.location: Mount of Beatitudes, Sea of Galilee
stories.hook: Jesus sat on a hillside overlooking the Sea of Galilee and delivered the most important speech in human history. Everything the world called blessed, He called backwards.
story: The Mount of Beatitudes is a rounded green hill on the northwestern shore of the Sea of Galilee, a freshwater lake in northern Israel sitting seven hundred feet below sea level. The lake is visible from the hill in almost its entirety — blue and shimmering on clear days, capable of sudden and violent storms on others. Jesus had been healing the sick, casting out demons, and preaching the kingdom of God throughout Galilee, and crowds had begun following Him from across the region. He saw the crowds, went up on a hillside, sat down — the traditional posture of a rabbi who was about to teach — and His disciples gathered around Him.

What He said next has shaped Western civilization, moral philosophy, and the spiritual imagination of billions of people for two thousand years. He began with what we call the Beatitudes — eight statements about who is truly blessed — and every one of them turned the values of the surrounding culture upside down. In first-century Jewish thought, God's blessing was commonly assumed to look like wealth, health, and social honor. Jesus said: Blessed are the poor in spirit. Blessed are those who mourn. Blessed are the meek. Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness. Blessed are the merciful. Blessed are the pure in heart. Blessed are the peacemakers. Blessed are those who are persecuted because of righteousness.

The word blessed — makarios in Greek — does not mean happy in the way we use the word today, as an emotional state depending on circumstances. It describes a condition of divine favor, of being genuinely well off in the deepest sense, regardless of what outward circumstances suggest. Jesus was not saying that the poor in spirit feel blessed. He was saying they are blessed — that God's favor rests on them, that the kingdom belongs to them.

He continued with teachings that pushed beneath the surface of the Law to reveal its inner logic. You have heard that it was said, Do not murder — and Jesus agreed. But then: I tell you that anyone who is angry with a brother or sister will be subject to judgment. He was not lowering the standard. He was revealing that the Law had always been about the heart, not just the behavior. God is not satisfied with people who manage not to commit murder while nursing hatred. He is after transformation — the kind that goes so deep that the desire to harm gives way to genuine love.

The same pattern repeated through several commandments. Do not commit adultery — yes, and anyone who looks at another person with lust has already committed adultery in the heart. Do not break your oath — better, let your yes be yes and your no be no. You have heard that it was said an eye for an eye — but Jesus said: Do not resist an evil person. If anyone slaps you on the right cheek, turn to them the other cheek also. This was not a call to passive victimhood. It was a call to break the cycle of escalating retaliation with something stronger than revenge.

And then the most radical instruction: You have heard that it was said, Love your neighbor and hate your enemy. But I tell you, love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you, that you may be children of your Father in heaven. The love Jesus was describing — agape in Greek — was not a feeling. It was a choice, a commitment, an action. God makes His sun rise on the evil and the good, and sends rain on the righteous and the unrighteous alike. The standard of love Jesus called His disciples to was the love of God — which extends to those who have no claim on it at all.

He turned to the interior life: prayer, fasting, giving. All three were common practices of Jewish piety — and all three could be performed for an audience or performed for God. When you give to the needy, do not announce it with trumpets. When you pray, do not stand on street corners to be seen by others. Your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you. The religious performance that impresses other people has already received its full reward — the impression of other people. That is all it will ever get.

He gave them a prayer — not as a formula to be recited mechanically but as a pattern for real conversation with God: acknowledgment of who God is and what His name deserves; submission to His kingdom and His will; honest daily requests for provision and forgiveness; a request for protection from temptation and deliverance from evil. The prayer assumes a relationship. It addresses God as Father — an intimate term that the religious establishment of the day would have found startling.

On money and anxiety: Do not store up for yourselves treasures on earth, where moths and vermin destroy, and where thieves break in and steal. And then the verse that has comforted more anxious hearts than perhaps any other: Do not worry about your life. Look at the birds of the air; they do not sow or reap or store away in barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them. Are you not much more valuable than they? The Father who notes the fall of a sparrow and numbers the hairs of your head is not indifferent to your needs. Worry accomplishes nothing — and it reveals an insufficient understanding of who the Father is.

The golden rule: In everything, do to others what you would have them do to you, for this sums up the Law and the Prophets. In a single sentence, Jesus summarized the entire ethical content of the Old Testament — making every subsequent moral complexity reducible to one question: what would I want if I were them?

He closed with the image of two builders. One heard His words and put them into practice — he was like a man who built his house on the rock. When the rain fell and the floods came and the winds blew and beat against the house, it did not fall, because it had its foundation on the rock. The other heard His words and did not put them into practice — he was like a man who built his house on sand. The same storm. The same conditions. Great was the fall of it. The difference was not talent or resources or intelligence. The difference was whether the words of Jesus became the foundation of the life.

When Jesus finished, the crowds were amazed at his teaching, because he taught as one who had authority, and not as their teachers of the law. The teachers of the Law quoted other authorities. Jesus quoted no one. He said: I tell you. He spoke as if the words originated with Him — because they did.

devotional.bigIdea: Jesus did not come to add religious duties to your life. He came to rebuild it from the foundation up, starting with the heart. The Sermon on the Mount is not a list of harder rules — it is a description of what a transformed life actually looks like.
questions: Which Beatitude is hardest for you to believe — that the person it describes is actually blessed? Why?
Jesus said anger is as serious as murder because God is after the heart, not just the behavior. How does that change the way you think about things you feel but do not act on?
The two builders heard the same words. The difference was whether they put them into practice. What is one thing from the Sermon on the Mount that you could actually do differently this week?

stories.title: The Boy Who Came Home
stories.hook: He demanded his inheritance early, spent every coin of it, and ended up starving in a foreign country. Then he came to himself — and started walking home.
story: Jesus told this parable on a specific occasion, to a specific audience, for a specific reason — and understanding all three makes the story cut deeper than it otherwise would. The occasion: the Pharisees and teachers of the law were grumbling because Jesus was welcoming tax collectors and sinners and eating with them. Eating with someone in that culture was not a casual act. It communicated acceptance, equality, relationship. By eating with people the religious establishment had written off, Jesus was making a theological claim: these people are not beyond reach. And the Pharisees hated it.

In response, Jesus told three stories about things that were lost and found. A lost sheep — the shepherd left the ninety-nine and searched until he found it. A lost coin — the woman swept the whole house until she found it. And then this — the longest of the three, the one that has broken and remade more hearts than almost any other story ever told.

A man had two sons. The younger came to his father with a request so insulting it is hard to overstate: Father, give me my share of the estate. In first-century Jewish culture, inheritance was distributed after a father's death. To ask for it while the father still lived was effectively to say: I wish you were dead. You are worth more to me gone than present. The father could have refused, rebuked, or disinherited the son on the spot. Instead, he divided his property between them. He gave the younger son what he asked for.

The younger son gathered everything, left for a distant country, and squandered his entire inheritance in wild living. The word prodigal does not mean rebellious — it means extravagant, wasteful, recklessly generous in the wrong direction. He burned through everything. Then a severe famine hit the country. He had nothing and no one — the friends that money had bought disappeared when the money did. He hired himself out to feed pigs. He was so hungry he was eyeing the pods the pigs were eating. And no one gave him anything.

Then — and this is the pivot on which the entire story turns — the text says he came to himself. He came to his senses. In the middle of a pig field in a foreign country, something cleared in his mind and he saw his situation honestly for the first time. His father's hired servants had more than enough bread. He was dying of hunger. He would go back. He would say: Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son. Make me like one of your hired servants. He was not expecting grace. He was hoping for employment. He rehearsed the speech on the long walk home.

The father saw him while he was still a long way off. That phrase has rearranged the theology of everyone who has read it carefully. While he was still a long way off. The father was looking. He had been looking every day since his son walked away — watching the road, watching the horizon, watching for the particular walk of his child among the travelers who came and went. He saw him. He recognized him at a distance. And he ran.

In the social world of first-century Palestine, a wealthy patriarch running was nearly unthinkable — it required gathering up your robes, exposing your legs, abandoning the dignity that social position required. He did it anyway. He ran to his son, threw his arms around him, and kissed him — the Greek word suggests repeated kissing, the extravagant embrace of someone who has been holding grief for a very long time and is suddenly, overwhelmingly, released from it.

The son started his rehearsed speech. Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son — The father did not let him finish. Before the request for hired-servant status could even be spoken, the father turned to his servants: Bring the best robe and put it on him. Put a ring on his finger and sandals on his feet. Bring the fattened calf and kill it. Let us have a feast and celebrate. Each item was significant: the best robe — honor, restoration. A ring — authority, family membership. Sandals — sons wore sandals; slaves did not. For this son of mine was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found.

The party began. And the older son came home from the field. He heard music and dancing and asked a servant what was happening. His brother has come home, the servant said. The older son was angry and refused to go in. His father came out and pleaded with him — notice again: the father going out, not waiting for his children to come to him.

The older son's response is understandable in a way that is deeply uncomfortable, because most of us recognize ourselves in it: Look! All these years I have been slaving for you and never disobeyed your orders. Yet you never gave me even a young goat so I could celebrate with my friends. But when this son of yours comes home, you kill the fattened calf for him! He called him this son of yours — not my brother. The resentment was old and deep.

The father's answer is one of the most beautiful and overlooked sentences in the parable: My son, you are always with me, and everything I have is yours. But we had to celebrate and be glad, because this brother of yours was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found. The older son had been living in the father's house all along. Everything the father had was available to him. What he had not done was claim it — had not realized that the father's presence was its own inheritance, worth more than any feast.

Jesus ended the story there — the door open, the music playing, the father waiting for the older son to decide whether to come in. He left it open because the story was addressed to the Pharisees standing right in front of him. They were the older brother. They had stayed, worked, obeyed — and resented the grace extended to people they had written off. Jesus was asking them: are you coming in, or are you staying out here in your resentment? The question has not changed.

devotional.bigIdea: The father ran before the son finished his confession. Before you have finished walking back, God is already running toward you. His love is not waiting for you to get yourself together. It runs.
questions: Which son are you more like right now — the one who ran away and wasted everything, or the one who stayed and got resentful? Be honest.
The father saw his son while he was still a long way off — he was watching and waiting. What does it mean to you that God sees you before you see Him?
The older brother stayed home and followed all the rules but missed the joy of the relationship with his father. Is it possible to be religiously faithful and still miss the point? How?

stories.title: Come Out
stories.hook: His friend had been dead for four days. Jesus wept. Then He told a dead man to walk out of his tomb.
story: Lazarus was sick. His sisters Mary and Martha sent a message to Jesus: Lord, the one you love is sick. The implied request was obvious. Come now. Jesus received the message and stayed where he was for two more days.

He told his disciples: This sickness will not end in death. It is for God's glory. Then after two days he said: Our friend Lazarus has fallen asleep; I am going to wake him up. They did not understand he meant death. He told them plainly: Lazarus is dead, and for your sake I am glad I was not there, so that you may believe.

By the time they arrived in Bethany, Lazarus had been in the tomb for four days. Martha came out to meet Jesus on the road. Lord, if you had been here, my brother would not have died. She said it without accusation, but with grief that was honest and total. Then she added: But I know that even now God will give you whatever you ask. Even now. After four days. Even now.

Jesus said: Your brother will rise again. Martha answered with careful, correct theology: I know he will rise again in the resurrection at the last day. Jesus answered with something that went beyond theology: I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in me will live, even though they die. Do you believe this? Martha said: Yes, Lord. I believe that you are the Messiah, the Son of God.

Mary came out and fell at Jesus's feet. She said the same words as her sister: Lord, if you had been here, my brother would not have died. And when Jesus saw her weeping, and the people weeping with her, he was deeply moved. Then the shortest verse in the Bible: Jesus wept.

They took him to the tomb — a cave with a stone rolled against it. Jesus said: Take away the stone. Martha said: Lord, by this time there is a bad odor, for he has been there four days. Jesus said: Did I not tell you that if you believe, you will see the glory of God? They took away the stone. Jesus prayed a short prayer of thanks. Then he called out: Lazarus, come out!
The dead man came out, his hands and feet wrapped with strips of linen, a cloth around his face. Take off the grave clothes and let him go. The crowd who saw it believed.

Jesus wept at the tomb of a man he was about to raise from the dead. He already knew what was going to happen. He wept anyway. Not because hope was gone, but because grief is real and loss is real and he was fully present to the pain of the people he loved. The one who is the resurrection and the life knows exactly what death costs.

devotional.bigIdea: Jesus wept at a tomb he was about to open. He does not stand outside your pain looking in — he enters it with you before He transforms it.
questions: Why do you think Jesus waited two extra days before going to Lazarus?
Jesus wept even though He knew what was about to happen. Why do you think He still cried?
Jesus asked Martha Do you believe this? before the miracle. Why does belief come before the miracle, not after?

stories.title: The Darkest Friday
stories.hook: They arrested Him in a garden. By midmorning He was nailed to a cross. By afternoon He was gone. And the world was never the same again.
story: The night before He died, Jesus walked from Jerusalem across the Kidron Valley to a garden called Gethsemane on the lower slopes of the Mount of Olives. Gethsemane means olive press — a place where olives were crushed between heavy stones to produce oil. The name was not accidental. He took Peter, James, and John deeper into the garden and told them what He was experiencing: My soul is overwhelmed with sorrow to the point of death. Stay here and keep watch with me. Then He went a little further and fell on His face.

What Jesus prayed in Gethsemane is one of the most theologically profound moments in all of Scripture. My Father, if it is possible, may this cup be taken from me. Yet not as I will, but as you will. He was not pretending the cross did not matter. The physical suffering of crucifixion was real and horrific. But the cup Jesus was asking about was more than physical pain — it was the full weight of human sin, the wrath of God against every transgression of every person who had ever lived or would ever live, about to be poured out onto the only sinless person in human history. He prayed this three times. Each time He returned to the disciples, they were asleep.

Luke's account adds a detail that stops you cold: in His agony, Jesus prayed more earnestly, and His sweat was like drops of blood falling to the ground. This condition — hematidrosis — can occur under extreme psychological stress when tiny blood vessels in the sweat glands rupture. He was not numb to what was coming. He knew exactly what the next twelve hours would bring, and He chose it anyway. An angel appeared from heaven and strengthened Him. He was fully God and fully man — and in His full humanity, He needed strengthening for what He was about to face.

Judas arrived with a crowd — soldiers and officials from the chief priests, carrying torches and weapons. Judas identified Jesus with a kiss on the cheek, the common greeting between a rabbi and his disciples. The most intimate gesture of relationship became the mechanism of betrayal. Jesus healed the ear of the high priest's servant struck by Peter's sword and told Peter to put his sword away: Do you think I cannot call on my Father, and he will at once put at my disposal more than twelve legions of angels? Seventy-two thousand angels. He was not being captured. He was surrendering. There is an enormous difference.

Six trials followed through the rest of the night and into the morning — before Annas the former high priest, before Caiaphas the current high priest and the Sanhedrin, before Pilate the Roman governor, before Herod Antipas, and twice more before Pilate. The Jewish leadership wanted the death penalty, which required Roman authorization. Pilate examined Jesus three times and declared three times that he found no basis for a charge against Him. He tried to release Jesus under the Passover custom of releasing one prisoner. The crowd chose Barabbas — a convicted insurrectionist and murderer — instead.

Pilate had Jesus flogged — a brutal punishment with a leather whip embedded with shards of bone and lead, designed to shred the skin of the back. The soldiers then dressed Jesus in a purple robe, twisted together a crown of thorns and pushed it onto His head, and mocked Him as a king. They struck Him again and again. Pilate brought Jesus out and said to the crowd: Here is the man. The chief priests shouted: Crucify! Crucify! The leaders of the people of God, the nation that had been waiting for their true King for a thousand years, declared that they had no king but Caesar.

They made Jesus carry His cross through the streets of Jerusalem. He collapsed under the weight, and a man named Simon of Cyrene was pressed into service to carry it the rest of the way. The destination was Golgotha — Place of the Skull — a rocky outcropping just outside the city walls, visible from the road, chosen specifically because executions were meant to be seen. Public. Humiliating. A warning.

They crucified Him at nine in the morning. Crucifixion was designed to be slow, agonizing, and degrading. Death typically came from suffocation as the position of the body made breathing increasingly difficult across the hours. From the cross, Jesus spoke seven times. His first words: Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing. To a criminal crucified beside him who asked to be remembered: Truly I tell you, today you will be with me in paradise. To His mother and the disciple John, He entrusted them to each other's care.

At noon, darkness covered the whole land — not an eclipse, which would have been impossible at Passover when the moon was full. Three hours of supernatural darkness, from noon to three in the afternoon. Then Jesus cried out: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? This is the opening line of Psalm 22 — a psalm of suffering that ends in deliverance and praise. Jesus was voicing the full weight of what atonement required: the One who knew no sin was becoming sin on our behalf, bearing our separation from God so that we would never have to.

He said He was thirsty. He received a sponge soaked in wine vinegar. Then He said: It is finished. In Greek: tetelestai. One word. The same word that merchants stamped on paid invoices in the ancient world: paid in full. Completed. Nothing owed. Nothing remaining. He bowed His head and gave up His spirit — John's phrasing suggests an act of will, not a collapse. At that moment, the curtain of the Temple that separated the Holy of Holies — the innermost room where God's presence dwelled, accessible only to the high priest, only once a year, only after elaborate ritual preparation — tore in two from top to bottom. From top to bottom. God tore it, not people. The barrier between God and humanity, established because of sin, was removed by the One who bore sin. The way in was open.

A Roman centurion who had overseen crucifixions throughout his career, who had watched many men die, stood at the foot of the cross and said: Surely this man was the Son of God. He had seen something he had no framework to explain. He was not wrong.

devotional.bigIdea: Tetelestai — it is finished — was the word stamped on paid invoices. Jesus was not announcing defeat. He was announcing that the debt had been paid in full, for everyone, forever. Nothing left owing.
questions: Jesus prayed not my will but yours in Gethsemane — fully God, and still choosing to submit. What does that tell you about what it means to trust God when the answer is hard?
His first words from the cross were a prayer for the forgiveness of the people killing him. That kind of love is not natural. Where does it come from, and how do you think Jesus sustained it?
The Temple curtain tore from top to bottom — God tore it, not people. What does it mean for your life that the barrier between you and God has been removed?

stories.title: The Empty Tomb
stories.hook: They buried Him on Friday. Sunday morning, the tomb was empty and the stone was moved — and then He appeared.
story: He was buried before sunset on Friday, because the Jewish Sabbath began at sundown and no work could be done on the Sabbath — not even burial preparations. Joseph of Arimathea, a wealthy member of the Jewish council who had been a secret follower of Jesus, asked Pilate for permission to take the body. Nicodemus, a Pharisee who had come to Jesus by night years earlier, brought about 75 pounds of myrrh and aloes. They wrapped Jesus's body in strips of linen with the spices, according to Jewish burial customs, and placed him in Joseph's own new tomb — a cave cut out of rock in a garden near Golgotha. A large stone was rolled across the entrance.

The chief priests and Pharisees went to Pilate the next day and asked for a guard to be placed at the tomb through the third day. They remembered that Jesus had said He would rise after three days, and they wanted to prevent the disciples from stealing the body and claiming a resurrection. Pilate gave them a guard. They sealed the stone and posted the soldiers. Every precaution that human authority could take to keep Jesus in the grave was taken.

Early on Sunday morning, while it was still dark, Mary Magdalene came to the tomb. She had been at the cross, watched where He was laid, and now came to grieve and complete the burial preparations cut short by the Sabbath. She saw that the stone had been taken away from the entrance. She ran immediately to Simon Peter and to the disciple whom Jesus loved — John — and told them: They have taken the Lord out of the tomb, and we do not know where they have put him.

Peter and John ran to the tomb. John arrived first. He bent down and looked in, saw the strips of linen lying there, but did not go in. Peter arrived, went straight past John, and entered. The burial cloths were lying there — not scattered or thrown aside as if someone had unwrapped a body in a hurry. Lying there. And the cloth that had been wrapped around Jesus's head was folded up by itself, separate from the linen. The detail of the folded face cloth is the kind of thing an eyewitness remembers and a fabricator would not think to invent. A body stolen in the night does not leave neatly folded grave clothes. John saw this and believed.

They went home. Mary stayed outside the tomb, weeping. She bent down to look in and saw two angels in white, seated where Jesus's body had been. They asked: Woman, why are you crying? She said: They have taken my Lord away, and I do not know where they have put him. She turned around and saw Jesus standing there, but she did not recognize Him. She thought He was the gardener. He asked: Woman, why are you crying? Who is it you are looking for? She said: Sir, if you have carried him away, tell me where you have put him, and I will get him. And then He said her name. Just her name: Mary. She turned to Him and said in Aramaic: Rabboni! — which means Teacher.

Jesus told her to go and tell His brothers: I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God. Mary Magdalene went to the disciples with the news: I have seen the Lord. She was the first person to carry the message of the resurrection. A woman whose testimony was not legally recognized in a first-century Jewish court was chosen by the risen Christ to be the first witness. Once again, God chose the unexpected messenger.

That evening, the disciples were gathered behind locked doors, afraid of the Jewish leaders. Jesus came and stood among them — present in the room in a way that transcended the physical limitations that had governed His pre-resurrection body. He showed them His hands and His side — the nail wounds and the spear wound, real, visible, touchable. He was not a ghost or a vision. He was physically present. He breathed on them and said: Receive the Holy Spirit.

Thomas had not been there. When the others told him they had seen the Lord, he said: Unless I see the nail marks in his hands and put my finger where the nails were, and put my hand into his side, I will not believe. A week later, the disciples were inside again and Thomas was with them. The doors were locked. Jesus came and stood among them. He went directly to Thomas: Put your finger here; see my hands. Reach out your hand and put it into my side. Stop doubting and believe. Thomas answered: My Lord and my God. Eight words that constitute the most direct declaration of Jesus's divinity in the entire Gospel of John, spoken by the man who had been most resistant to believing.

Jesus said: Because you have seen me, you have believed; blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed. Those words were not a rebuke of Thomas — they were a promise for everyone who would come after, everyone who would read this account without the ability to stand in that room and touch the wounds themselves. Blessed. That blessing is for you.

Over forty days, Jesus appeared multiple times in multiple locations. He appeared to Mary Magdalene. He appeared to two disciples on the road to Emmaus. He appeared to Peter individually — an encounter that must have been profound given Peter's three denials, and that John records as a careful, three-part restoration by the sea: Do you love me? Three denials, three restorations, three commissions: Feed my sheep. He appeared to more than five hundred people at one time, Paul notes in his first letter to the Corinthians — written within twenty-five years of the events, when most of those witnesses were still alive and could be questioned.

The resurrection is not a metaphor. It is not a spiritual truth wrapped in a fictional story. It is a historical claim — the most extraordinary claim ever made — that a man who was publicly executed, certified dead, and buried in a sealed and guarded tomb was physically, bodily, alive again three days later. Paul understood the stakes clearly: If Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile; you are still in your sins. The resurrection is not a supporting detail of Christianity. It is the foundation on which everything else stands. If it happened — and the evidence, including the empty tomb, the eyewitness accounts, the transformation of the disciples, and the conversion of Paul — is substantial, then every promise Jesus made is guaranteed. Every word He spoke is authoritative. Every claim He made about Himself is true. Death is not the end. The tomb is empty. He is risen.

devotional.bigIdea: Paul said it plainly: if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile. But if He has — and He has — then death is not the end, every promise He made stands, and nothing you face is beyond His reach.
questions: Jesus said Mary's name and she immediately recognized Him. What does it mean to you that the risen Christ calls you by name?
Thomas said he would not believe without seeing, and Jesus showed up for him without a word of rebuke. What does that tell you about how Jesus responds to honest doubt?
The resurrection is the foundation of everything. If you really believed it — not just as information but as a fact that changes everything — what would be different about how you live tomorrow?

stories.title: The Day Everything Changed
stories.hook: Fifty days after Passover, the disciples were together in a room when wind filled the house and fire appeared above their heads.
story: Jesus had told his disciples to wait in Jerusalem. He had promised them the Holy Spirit — and that they would receive power and would be his witnesses to the ends of the earth. Then he ascended. They went back to Jerusalem and waited. About a hundred and twenty of them, including the disciples and Mary the mother of Jesus and his brothers, prayed and waited for ten days.

The Jewish festival of Pentecost arrived — fifty days after Passover, a celebration of the first harvest and traditionally the giving of the Law at Sinai. Suddenly a sound like the blowing of a violent wind came from heaven and filled the whole house. What looked like tongues of fire appeared and rested on each of them. All of them were filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other languages.

Jerusalem was packed with Jewish pilgrims from every nation who had come for Pentecost. The sound drew a crowd. They were bewildered because each one heard their own language being spoken. Parthians, Medes, people from Mesopotamia, Egypt, Rome, Crete, and Arabia — all hearing the wonders of God in their own native tongue. What does this mean? some asked. Others mocked: They have had too much wine.

Peter stood up — this was Peter, who weeks earlier had denied three times that he even knew Jesus, cowering by a fire in the high priest's courtyard. He was transformed. He explained what they were seeing, walked them through the entire story of Israel pointing to Jesus, and landed on the resurrection as the hinge point of everything.

Therefore let all Israel be assured of this, Peter said. God has made this Jesus, whom you crucified, both Lord and Messiah. The crowd was cut to the heart. Brothers, what shall we do? Peter said: Repent and be baptized, every one of you, in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins. And you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit.
About three thousand people were baptized that day. The church that would carry the message of Jesus to every corner of the world was born in Jerusalem on the day the Spirit came like fire.

The same disciples who had been hiding behind locked doors were now preaching in the streets. The difference was not courage they had worked up. The difference was that they were no longer doing it alone.

The Spirit that had rested on prophets and kings in the Old Testament was now given to every believer — ordinary people, in every language, from every nation. The ends of the earth had been the assignment. It is still happening.

devotional.bigIdea: The same Spirit that raised Jesus from the dead lives in every person who follows him. You are not doing this alone.
questions: Peter had denied Jesus three times just weeks before. What changed in him between the courtyard and Pentecost?
Three thousand people responded in a single day. What does that tell you about what happens when the Spirit moves?
Jesus promised the Holy Spirit would be a Helper and give power. What does that mean for your life day to day?

stories.title: The Man Who Hunted Christians
stories.hook: He was on his way to arrest Christians when a blinding light knocked him down and God spoke his name.
story: Saul of Tarsus was a zealot in the truest sense. A Pharisee, trained by the greatest rabbi in Jerusalem, impeccably educated in Jewish law, intensely dedicated to God — and completely convinced that the movement growing up around Jesus was a dangerous heresy that had to be destroyed. He had stood watching approvingly as a crowd stoned the first Christian martyr, Stephen, to death. He then went house to house in Jerusalem, dragging men and women to prison.

When the Jerusalem church scattered under the persecution, Saul went after them. He asked the high priest for letters to the synagogues in Damascus — authorization to arrest any followers of the Way and bring them bound to Jerusalem. He set out with a group of companions.

About noon, as they approached Damascus, a light from heaven flashed around him. Brighter than the noonday sun — the text says this explicitly. He fell to the ground. And a voice said: Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me? Saul asked: Who are you, Lord? The answer: I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting. Now get up and go into the city, and you will be told what you must do.
The men with him stood speechless — they heard the sound but saw no one. Saul got up. He was blind. They led him by the hand into Damascus, where he did not eat or drink for three days.

In Damascus, there was a disciple named Ananias. The Lord appeared to him in a vision and told him to go to Saul. Ananias answered with what anyone would have answered: Lord, I have heard many reports about this man and all the harm he has done to your people. He has come here with authority to arrest all who call on your name. The Lord replied: Go. This man is my chosen instrument to proclaim my name to the Gentiles and their kings and to the people of Israel.

Ananias went. He placed his hands on Saul and said: Brother Saul, the Lord Jesus, who appeared to you on the road, has sent me so that you may see again and be filled with the Holy Spirit. Immediately something like scales fell from Saul's eyes and he could see again. He was baptized.

The man who had come to Damascus to arrest Christians left Damascus preaching that Jesus was the Son of God. The synagogues were astonished: Is not this the man who raised havoc in Jerusalem among those who call on this name? He became Paul — the apostle who would plant churches across the Roman empire and write nearly half the New Testament.

Paul would later write: I was shown mercy so that in me, the worst of sinners, Christ Jesus might display his immense patience as an example for those who would believe in him. If God can reach Paul on the road to Damascus, He can reach anyone, anywhere, doing anything.

devotional.bigIdea: Nobody is too far gone, too opposed, or too certain they are right to be reached by God. The story of Paul is proof.
questions: Paul was completely sure he was doing the right thing by persecuting Christians. What does that tell you about the danger of certainty without humility?
Jesus asked Paul why do you persecute me — identifying completely with the people Paul was hurting. What does that tell you about how Jesus feels about how we treat other people?
Paul said he was shown mercy as an example for everyone who would come after. Is there someone in your life you have written off as too far from God?

stories.title: Singing at Midnight
stories.hook: Paul and Silas were beaten, thrown in the deepest part of a prison, and chained to the wall. Then they started singing.
story: Paul and his companion Silas were in Philippi — a major Roman city in what is now northern Greece, one of the first European cities to hear the message of Jesus. They had been followed for days by a slave girl who had a spirit that enabled her to predict the future, which made her owners a lot of money. Paul, deeply troubled, commanded the spirit in the name of Jesus Christ to leave her. And it did.

Her owners were furious. Their source of income was gone. They grabbed Paul and Silas, dragged them to the marketplace, and accused them before the magistrates: These men are Jews, and are throwing our city into an uproar by advocating customs unlawful for us Romans to accept. The crowd joined in the attack. The magistrates ordered them stripped and beaten with rods — a serious Roman punishment — and thrown into prison.
The jailer was commanded to guard them carefully. He put them in the inner cell, the most secure and most miserable part of the prison, and fastened their feet in stocks so they could not move.

It was midnight. Paul and Silas were in the deepest part of a Roman prison, their backs torn from the beating, their feet locked in stocks, probably in darkness. And they were praying and singing hymns to God. The other prisoners were listening.

Suddenly there was such a violent earthquake that the foundations of the prison were shaken. At once all the prison doors flew open, and everyone's chains came loose. The jailer woke up and saw the prison doors open. Assuming the prisoners had escaped, he drew his sword to kill himself — a Roman guard who lost his prisoners was subject to whatever penalty those prisoners would have received. Before he could do it, Paul shouted: Do not harm yourself! We are all here!

The jailer called for lights, rushed in, and fell trembling before Paul and Silas. He brought them out and asked: Sirs, what must I do to be saved? They replied: Believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved — you and your household. They spoke the word of the Lord to him and to all the others in his house.

At that hour of the night, the jailer took them and washed their wounds. Then immediately he and all his household were baptized. He brought them into his house, set a meal before them, and he and his whole household were filled with joy, because they had come to believe in God.

Paul and Silas had been beaten and imprisoned for doing exactly what God had called them to do. They sang anyway. And the earthquake that opened the prison doors opened a family to God at midnight. What feels like the worst situation is sometimes exactly the right location.

devotional.bigIdea: Paul and Silas sang in prison not because the situation was good, but because God was still God. Worship is not a response to circumstances — it is a declaration that God is bigger than circumstances.
questions: Paul and Silas sang in prison after being beaten. What do you think made that possible?
The jailer was about to kill himself when Paul stopped him. What does it say about Paul's character that even in chains he was paying attention to someone else's desperation?
Have you ever had a situation that felt completely wrong turn into something unexpectedly good? What happened?
